title: Simple Porch Swing
summary: A four-foot porch swing built like a small bench — 2x4 frame, 1x6 slats, no pocket holes required. The hanging hardware is the part you don't improvise.
description:
A porch swing is a bench with a trust problem: every joint in it hangs in the air with people on it. This design earns that trust the simple way — a rectangular 2x4 seat frame, back supports screwed to it, arms tied into both — with nothing exotic. Pocket holes are optional; the plan even includes the workaround if you don't have a jig (cut the back boards 3-1/2" longer and skip the top back rail).

The build is the easy half. The hanging is the half to be careful with: use rated eye bolts or a porch swing hanging kit — through-bolted to the swing frame, never lag-screwed into end grain — and hang it from ceiling joists or a beam, never from ceiling boards. Four hang points (both ends of seat and back rails) keep the swing from folding forward when someone sits on the front edge. A 4-foot swing with two adults is easily 400+ lbs of live, moving load; every component in the chain should be rated well past it.

Cedar or pre-finished pine both work. Whatever the species, finish it before hanging — a hung swing is a miserable thing to paint the underside of.

tools.note: Pilot holes at every board end, and the through-holes for the hanging bolts.
Optional — cleaner joints on the frame and top back rail. The plan includes the no-jig workaround.
Every surface gets touched on a swing. Sand like it's furniture, because it is.

materials.name: Exterior screws, #9 x 2-1/2", self-tapping
materials.note: Frame assembly — or 2-1/2" exterior pocket screws if using a jig.

materials.name: Porch swing hanging kit or rated eye bolts and chain
materials.note: Rated hardware, through-bolted. This line item is not the place to save eight dollars.

cutList.part: Seat frame, front and back rails

cutList.note: No pocket hole jig? Cut these 3-1/2" longer (23-1/4") and omit the top back rail.

steps.title: Build the seat frame
steps.body:
Assemble the basic rectangle: front and back 48" rails with the 18" ends and two interior supports between them. Pocket screws if you have the jig, otherwise 2-1/2" screws through the rails into the crosspieces, pilot-drilled.

Everything on this swing hangs from this frame. Square it, and don't skimp a screw.

steps.title: Add the back supports and top rail
steps.body:
Screw the four back supports to the seat frame, then tie their tops together with the 48" top rail.

No jig? Use the plan's alternative: back supports cut 3-1/2" longer, no top rail — the back slats themselves tie the tops together instead.

steps.title: Attach the front arm supports
steps.body: Screw the two short arm supports vertically to the front corners of the seat frame — pilot-drill, these are near board ends.

steps.title: Land the arms
steps.body: Set each arm on its front support and screw it there and into the back support. Check the two arms are level with each other — on a swing your eye catches an arm mismatch instantly, because the horizon is right behind it.

steps.title: Slat the seat
steps.body: Screw the 1x6 seat slats across the frame with the 1-1/2" screws, small even gaps for drainage. Round the front slat's edge generously — it's the one behind everyone's knees.

steps.title: Slat the back
steps.body: Attach the remaining 1x6s and the two 1x4s to the back supports, even gaps. The mixed widths give the back a subtle rhythm — lay them out and look before you screw.

steps.title: Finish, then hang it right
steps.body:
Sand thoroughly and finish every face — including the underside, which is at eye level to everyone walking up your porch steps.

Hang with a rated kit or rated eye bolts: THROUGH-BOLT the hardware to the seat frame (front and back rail at each end, four points total), and fasten overhead only into joists or a structural beam. Test-load it with more weight than it will ever carry before a person swings on it. Chain angle slightly outward at the arms keeps it from twisting.
materials: Porch swing hanging kit or rated eye bolts and chain

images.alt: Wooden porch swing with slatted seat and back hanging from chains
